---
title: "Example Usage"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{example-usage}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
```

```{r setup}
#devtools::load_all()
library(fatherStay)
library(ggplot2)
```

We'll start by simulating some data from a mechanistic model. The `simulate_mechanistic()` function generates a data frame with a single time series, with the response variable `dad` and covariates `cnisp`, `ptsos`, and `npos`. The data frame also include a `date` column. The last 10 observations are NA, which will be used for nowcasting.

```{r make_data}
df <- simulate_mechanistic()
tail(df, 12) # last 10 are NA - to be nowcast!
pairs(df[, -1])
```

The basic usage of the package involves the "nowcast" function, which fits a single nowcasting model to the data. 

The formula interface is used to specify the response variable and covariates, and lags can be specified using the "lag" function within the formula. The function converts the data into a standard format across all models so that a single "multidadnow" object can be used to store the results of multiple models, and the same plotting and evaluation functions can be used regardless of the underlying model.

The `add_nowcast()` interface allows for new formulas to be specified when adding new models. Notice that a message will be displayed if no formula is specified, in whiuch case the formula from the first registered model will be used.


```{r nowcast}
dadnow2 <- nowcast(
  formula = dad ~ lag(cnisp, 3) + lag(ptsos, 3),
  data = df, 
  model = "ar", 
  date_col = "date", 
  params = list(order = 1)
)

dadnow2 <- add_nowcast(dadnow2, model = "ar", params = list(order = 2)) |>
  add_nowcast("lm") |>
  add_nowcast("lm", formula = dad ~ cnisp + ptsos + npos) |>
  add_nowcast("arx", params = list(order = 1)) |>
  add_nowcast("arx", formula = dad ~ npos, params = list(order = 1)) |>
  add_nowcast("arx", formula = dad ~ npos, params = list(order = 2)) |>
  add_nowcast("gam", params = list(smooths = list("cnisp_lag0"=10))) |>
  add_nowcast("rf", formula = dad ~ lag(cnisp, 2), params = list(ntree = 50, mtry = 3))

dadnow2 <- add_nowcast(dadnow2, model = "xgboost", params = list(nrounds = 10))

dadnow2
```


There's also an `autoplot()` method for "multidadnow" objects, which plots the nowcast against the observed data. By default, it plots all training data in the "multidadnow" object, but the `last_n` argument can be used to specify how many of the most recent observations to plot.

The function uses the same data as the output of `get_data()`, so the data can be extracted from the "multidadnow" object and used in the plot.

```{r autoplot}
autoplot(dadnow2, last_n = 25, se = TRUE) +
  theme_bw() +
  facet_wrap(formula ~ model)

```

The evaluations can easily be extracted and plotted:

```{r}
evals <- get_evals(dadnow2)

ggplot(evals) +
  aes(x = reorder(model_id, mae), y = mae, fill = model) +
  geom_col() +
  theme_bw()
```

The package includes a "mechanistic" model, which can be seen in `vignette("Mechanistic_Model", package = "fatherStay")`. The model takes the parameters `sc` and `sp` and requires a method ("normal", "poisson", or "negbinom") to specify the distribution of the error term. Because it works slightly differently than the other models, it is added to a "multidadnow" object using the `add_mechanistic` function, which takes the same formula interface as `nowcast` but with different parameters. The mechanistic model can be added alongside the other models, and the same plotting and evaluation functions can be used.

Note that the mechanistic assumption is sensitive to the values of sc and sp, and there will often be warnings about lack of convergence in the optimization process.

```{r}
mech <- nowcast(
  model = "mechanistic",
  dad ~ cnisp + ptsos + npos, df, date_col = "date",
  params = list(sc = 0.2, sp = 0.3, method = "poisson")
)
mech
```

In addition to `add_nowcast()` and `add_mechanistic()`, which adds a single model to a "multidadnow" object, there is also a `combine_nowcasts` function, which combines two "multidadnow" objects.

```{r}
dadnow3 <- combine_nowcasts(dadnow2, mech) |>
  add_nowcast(formula = dad ~ cnisp + ptsos + npos, model = "mechanistic", params = list(sc = 0.2, sp = 0.3, method = "normal")) |>
  add_nowcast(formula = dad ~ cnisp + ptsos + npos, model = "mechanistic", params = list(sc = 0.2, sp = 0.3, method = "poisson"))

dadnow3
autoplot(dadnow3, last_n = 5)
```

The `add_nowcast()` interface allows for new formulas to be specified when adding new models. 


```{r}
library(patchwork)
gg_ptsos <- ggplot(df, aes(x = date, y = ptsos)) + geom_line()
gg_cnisp <- ggplot(df, aes(x = date, y = cnisp)) + geom_line()

gg_dadnow <- autoplot(dadnow2, last_n = 150) +
  theme_bw()

gg_dadnow / gg_ptsos / gg_cnisp
```

The data can be extracted from the "multidadnow" object using the `get_predictions()` and `get_model()` functions.

The data can be extracted from the "multidadnow" object in order to check for patterns in the predictions:

```{r}
train_preds <- get_data(dadnow2, include_training = TRUE)

gg_npos <- ggplot(train_preds, aes(x = npos, y = dad, colour = model_id)) + geom_point()
gg_ptsos <- ggplot(train_preds, aes(x = ptsos, y = dad, colour = model_id)) + geom_point()
gg_cnisp <- ggplot(train_preds, aes(x = cnisp, y = dad, colour = model_id)) + geom_point()

gg_npos + gg_ptsos + gg_cnisp + plot_layout(ncol = 2, guides = "collect")
```

And residual plots for the training data can be made as follows. Note that there's no concept of residuals for nowcasts since we don't have an observed y value. If desired, the enbpi function could be changed to output all of the predictions and residuals for more fine-grained inspection of how each model is fitting.

```{r}
resids <- get_residuals(dadnow2)

ggplot(resids, aes(x = fitted_value, y = residual, colour = model_id)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~ model_id)
```


## Evaluation Metrics

The evaluation metrics come from the `fatherStay:::enbpi()` function, which uses Ensemble batch prediction intervals to calculate prediction intervals and prediction metrics. This works by creating many different "batches" (which are contiguous sets of data, i.e., using all data in a time interval), each with the same number of training observations; fitting a model to the batch; and then predicting $k$ steps ahead ($k$ is chosen based on the amount of trailing NAs in DAD data, i.e., the number of nowcasts to be made). For example, if there are 10 points in the data to be nowcast and you choose `batches=40` with `training_window=NULL`, then the function will create 40 batches, each with 60% of the training data, fit a model, then calculate the 10-step ahead predictions from each model. The batches all have the same size but a random starting index, meaning that we get data without time jumps.

From each batch, we have a set of predictions compared to actual values. The standard deviation of the 1-step ahead prediction can be calculated from the batches, then the standard deviation of the 2-step ahead, etc. This gives us an empirical measure of the prediction intervals for our particular nowcasting scenario.

This is similar to k-fold cross-validation, but targeted to a nowcasting scenario. With k-fold CV, the data into k folds and we predict all of the data from each fold. This does not take the ordering of the data into account, and the evaluation metrics are based on the amounts of data in each fold (for example, in 10-fold CV, each fold has 1/10 of the data). EnBPI takes the ordering into account and also finds metrics (RMSE, MAE, and MRE) based on exactly the sort of predictions that we are making.

- More `batch`es means more accurate prediction intervals, but also more computationally intensive.
- The default `train_window` uses 60% of the training data is used for each batch, leaving space for a variety of intervals while also using enough of the data for training.
    - To see why, consider the case where we have 100 data points, 5-step ahead predictions, and we use 95% of the data in each batch. Since we need space to do 5-step ahead predictions, every single batch is going to be the first 95 data points! If we used 60% instead, then the batches will have starting indices anywhere between 0 and 45, so we have a greater variety of k-step ahead predictions.
    - If we used 5% of the data in each batch, we would be training on only 5 data points each time, which is not a good reflection of the amount of data that we actually have.
- The `level` is the prediction interval level. The prediction intervals are calculated based on an asymptotic normality assumption: the standard error ($SE$) is caclulated empirically, then the interval is calculated as $\hat y \pm z_{(1 - \texttt{level})/2} SE$.

The parameters for EnBPI can be specified in the nowcast function, then `add_nowcast()` will read those parameters and use them for any additional nowcasts. The defaults are shown below in `dadnow4`. If two sets of `multidadnow` objects have different values for `batches`, `train_window`, or `level`, then `combine_nowcasts()` will fail since the results are not comparable. 

```{r}
system.time(
  dadnow4 <- nowcast(
    dad ~ lag(cnisp, 3) + lag(ptsos, 3),
    df, model = "ar", date_col = "date", params = list(order = 1),
    batches = 40, train_window = NULL, level = 0.95
  )
) |> print()

system.time(
  dadnow5 <- nowcast(
    dad ~ lag(cnisp, 3) + lag(ptsos, 3),
    df, model = "ar", date_col = "date", params = list(order = 1),
    batches = 100, train_window = NULL, level = 0.95
  )
)

tryCatch(
  combine_nowcasts(dadnow4, dadnow5),
  error = function(e) e
)
```



## Further Details

A "multidadnow" object is just a list, where certain information that is common to all models are at the highest level, and the models themselves are stored in a list within the object.

```{r}
names(dadnow2)

```

```{r}
names(dadnow2$models)
```

It is most convenient to use the `get_model()` and `get_predictions()` functions to extract information from the "multidadnow" object, but you can also directly access the list elements.

```{r}
dadnow2$models[["f1_lm"]]$model
get_model(dadnow2, "f1_lm")
```
